coredata.dc:title: Environmental synthesis of yellow fluorescent carbon dots for on-off-on detection of mercury and cysteine
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Dan
Wang, Qin
Zhao, Shaojing
Yi, Jianing
Lan, Minhuan
Zeng, Jie
Huang, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Herein, carbon dots (CDs) with bright yellow fluorescence were synthesized by using kaempferol as a carbon source, through a hydrothermal method in NaOH solution. The as-prepared CDs were highly soluble and spherical with uniform size and mean particle diameter of 3.2 nm. The CDs have unique high selectivity, sensitivity and anti-interference ability to mercury (Hg2+). Under different pH conditions, Hg2+ ions can still quench the fluorescence of CDs effectively and stably. On the other hand, cysteine can re-ignite the fluorescence of CDs as a result of strong chelation interaction that exists between -SH and Hg2+. There are good linear relationships between the concentration of Hg2+ and (F0-F)/F0, the concentration of cysteine and (F-F0)/F0, and the detection limits are 1.6 μM and 0.8 μM, respectively. In addition, CDs can effectively detect Hg2+ in tap water and lake water and detect cysteine in serum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent probe
Kaempferol-based carbon dots
On-off–on detection
Mercury ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CDs- Hg2+ application for sensing Cys
CDs-Hg2+ for sensing cysteine as a fluorescence turn-on sensor
Analysis of Hg2+ in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs. (b) Raman spectroscopy, (c) FT-IR spectra, (d) XPS survey spectrum, and high-resolution XPS spectra of (e) C1s and (f) O1s of the CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs. (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (c) Fluorescence spectra of CDs on the gradual addition of Hg2+ from 0 to 50 μM. (d) Liner plot of (F0-F)/F0 versus the concentration of Hg2+.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs aqueous solutions in the presence of different metal ions(50 μM). (b) Fluorescence intensity ratio of (F0-F)/F0 at 543 nm of CDs at pH 7 in the presence of metal ions(50 μM) before and after adding Hg2+(50 μM), and (c) in different Hg2+ concentrations (10, 20, and 30 μM) versus time. (d) Influence of pH on the fluorescent intense at 543 nm of CDs and CDs-Hg2+ solutions.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs-Hg2+ aqueous solution upon gradual addition of Cys from 0 to 70 μM. (b) Line graph of (F0-F)/F0 versus the concentration of Cys. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs-Hg2+ in buffer solution with 70 μM Cys. (d) Fluorescence spectra of CDs-Hg2+ solution with different amino acids(70 μM). (e) Time-dependent of the (F0-F)/F0 ratio of CDs-Hg2+ in 70 μM Cys concentrations. (f) Fluorescence intensity ratio of (F-F0)/F0 at 543 nm of CDs − Hg2+ in the presence of different amino acids(70 μM) before and after adding Cys(70 μM).
Synthesis process and detection mechanism of PT-CDs.
Analytical results for Hg2+ detection in river water and tap water.

head.ce:title: Environmental synthesis of yellow fluorescent carbon dots for on-off-on detection of mercury and cysteine

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, carbon dots (CDs) with bright yellow fluorescence were synthesized by using kaempferol as a carbon source, through a hydrothermal method in NaOH solution. The as-prepared CDs were highly soluble and spherical with uniform size and mean particle diameter of 3.2 nm. The CDs have unique high selectivity, sensitivity and anti-interference ability to mercury (Hg2+). Under different pH conditions, Hg2+ ions can still quench the fluorescence of CDs effectively and stably. On the other hand, cysteine can re-ignite the fluorescence of CDs as a result of strong chelation interaction that exists between -SH and Hg2+. There are good linear relationships between the concentration of Hg2+ and (F0-F)/F0, the concentration of cysteine and (F-F0)/F0, and the detection limits are 1.6 μM and 0.8 μM, respectively. In addition, CDs can effectively detect Hg2+ in tap water and lake water and detect cysteine in serum.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury （Hg2+）is known as one of the major harmful substances in public health issues [1–3]. Since mercury ions are not biodegradable, the accumulation of Hg2+ ions will cause serious and irreversible damage to human health in the human body through the food chain, so the prevention and control of mercury poisoning have become critical issues faced by all countries in the world [4–6]. Therefore, it is relevant to consider a simple, economical, and highly selective analytical approach for Hg2+ sensing. There generally exist several determination methods of Hg2+, including atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GCMS), and anodic stripping voltammetry [7–10]. Nevertheless, these techniques require costly equipment, tedious sample pretreatment procedures, and unsuitable for field analysis. Compared with these methods, fluorescent probes have drawn more focus with low detection limits, simplicity, and potential applications in environmental chemistry and biology [11–14]. Organic dyes, conjugated polymers, semiconductor quantum dots, etc., have been designed for Hg2+ detection [15–18]. For example, Kumar et al. prepared six probes that can selectively bind Hg2+ ions by using the naphthoquinoline dione ring, which showed the color of naked eye turn from yellow to purple when bonded to Hg2+ ions [19]. Wang et al. constructed a chitosan-based polymer sensor by combining with fluorescent small-molecular BODIPY (BY-3) containing CC group through a spontaneous NH2/CC click reaction, which can effectively recognize Hg2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions [20]. In addition, Li et al. prepared silver-doped CdTe quantum dots encapsulated with L-cysteine to detect Hg2+ ions [21]. However, the cost of raw materials is high and the quantity is limited. Although there have been significant achievements in fluorescence probe detection, these probes suffer from complex preparation, low selectivity, and are not environmentally friendly.

On the other hand, Cysteine is a common amino acid in organisms. For human body, cysteine deficiency can lead to hair decolorization, lethargy, liver injury, skin injury, muscle and fat loss and other symptoms [22], while excessive cysteine has toxic effects on the body [23]. Therefore, it is of great significance to establish a rapid and efficient analytical method for the determination of cysteine.

As a novel fluorescent material, CDs have the strength of being easy to synthesize, unique optical properties, good photostability, and biocompatibility [24–27]. CDs are widely available for fluorescent sensing and imaging in biology [28–30]. For instance, Shili et al. designed a fluorescent composite probe, TpPa-1 COF @ CDs, which showed an excellent detection and removal capabilities for Hg2+ ions. However, the Hg2+ ions could only reduce the luminescence intensity of TpPa-1 COF @ CDs by 45 % [31]. Tan et al. used L-arginine as raw material to prepare CDs that were used to detect Cd2+ and Hg2+ ions in water, but the reaction time was as long as five minutes [32]. Fe3+ and Hg2+ ions can quench the fluorescence of N-S-CDs prepared by Liu et al. with little difference in quenching degree. Thus, an improvement in selectivity is required [33].

Natural carbon sources have attracted people's attention because of their sustainability, biocompatibility and inexpensive. Many CDs prepared from natural carbon sources have been used to detect metal ions in water. For example, Ansi et al. used edible sugar as carbon source to prepare carbon dots to detect Pb2+ in water [34]. Yu et al. used sweet-scented osmanthus to prepare a carbon dot for high sensitivity detection of quercetin and aluminum ions and cell imaging [35]. Raja et al. studied Curauá-derived carbon dots as fluorescent probes for detecting Fe (III) ion [36]. Kaempferol is a natural substance extracted from plant species, which is cheap and has no toxic effect on environment and organisms, so it has the potential to serve as an excellent source of carbon [37]. In this study, the authors synthesized CDs with bright yellow fluorescence by hydrothermal treatment of Kaempferol in aqueous NaOH solution for selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+ ions. As shown in Scheme 1 , the hydroxyl group on the surface of the synthesized CDs can interact with Hg2+ ions to form an aggregation/network structure, which leads to the quenching of fluorescence intensity [38,39]. The yield of CDs was 2.1 %. Because of the strong chelating interaction between-SH and Hg2+ [40]. cysteine can reignite the fluorescence of CDs. This “on–off–on” type of sensor could be applied in detecting Hg2 + and cysteine. Furthermore, CDs can be employed to detect the presence of Hg2 + ions in environmental water bodies, such as rivers and running water, with an acceptable recovery rate.

Kaempferol, NaOH, Na2HPO4, Citric Acid, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2·6H2O, MgCl2, ZnCl2, FeCl3·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, PbCl2, CdCl2, CoCl2·6H2O, BaCl2, HgCl2, lysine(Lys), cysteine(Cys), glutathione(GSH), homocysteine(Hcy), threonine(Thr), valine(Val), argine(Arg), methionine(Met), serine(Ser), Histidine(His), isoleucine(Ile), alanine(Ala), and aspartic acid(Asp), were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a Tecnai G2 20S-Twin electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was employed to analyze the infrared spectrum of absorption and emission of the samples on a FTIR8400S Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Tyoto, Japan). A Horiba HR 800 Raman system was used to evaluate the Raman spectra of the CDs and an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was performed with a KRATOS ULTRA-DLD (KRATOS Analytical) Analyzer. Also, a Zeta-ZS instrument (Malvern) was used to measure ζ-potentials. The luminescence lifetime was evaluated by an Edinburgh FLS 1000 instrument, while fluorescence was observed on an RF6000 spectrophotometer. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded by an UV-2000 UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

Firstly, kaempferol(30 mg) was dissolved in 100 mM NaOH solution. The solution was transferred to a 50 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) autoclave. After heating the solution to 180℃ for 10 h, the product is allowed to cool to room temperature. Afterwards, the product was thoroughly stirred with a centrifugal force, and filtered using a 0.22 µm microporous membrane to remove insoluble impurities. Further purification was done by using a 500-Da dialysis membrane for 36 h.

First, the CDs obtained were diluted with a configured buffer solution (pH 7.0) and used as fluorescent probes. Next, varying concentrations of Hg2+ were added to the mixture of CDs. The excitation wavelength used was 480 nm. For the analysis of Hg2+ in environmental water samples, 10 μM, 20 μM, 30 μM Hg2+ solution was prepared using river water and tap water. All of the measurements were repeated three times to ensure accuracy and reliability of the results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs- Hg2+ application for sensing Cys
ce:section.ce:para: A quantity of 50 μM of Hg2+ was added to the as-prepared CDs to synthesize a sensor (CDs-Hg2+), which was used for sensing cCysteine. Thereafter, different concentrations of Cysteine were introduced into the CDs-Hg2+ mixture. In order to confirm the feasibility of the probe in detecting cysteine in real samples, 20 μM, 40 μM, 60 μM Cys solution was prepared by using serum diluted 50 times. Meanwhile, all the above tests were carried out in the optimum buffer pH value and repeated three times to ensure the reliability and accuracy of data.

TEM was used to explore the size and morphology of synthesised CDs. The TEM image demonstrated monodisperse that were spherical and uniform in size(Fig. 1 a). The particle size of CDs mainly ranged from 2.5 nm to 3.8 nm, with an average particle size of 3.2 nm (Fig. S1). The Raman spectrum of the CDs showed two peaks at 1317 and 1569 cm−1, representing the D and G bands of graphite carbon, respectively (Fig. 1b). The ID/IG ratio of the CDs was 1.16, which indicated the presence of a significant amount of structural defects in the CDs [41]. Subsequently, the FTIR spectra of the CDs were analyzed to gain more insight on their surface chemical bonding (Fig. 1c). The strong absorption peak at 3045 cm−1 was caused by OH vibration, indicating the abundance of hydroxyl groups present in the CDs. The clear peak at 1575 cm−1 was indicative of the tensile vibration of CO bonds, while the two sharp peaks at 1521 cm−1 and 1455 cm−1 represented the flexural vibration of the CC bonds. Moreso, the absorption peak at 1267 cm−1 was associated with the tensile vibration of the CO bonds. The peak at 2911 cm−1 corresponds to the CH bond. In addition, the FTIR spectra of the CDs were also analyzed after the addition of Hg2+ ions. (Fig. S2) The FTIR spectra of the CDs, before and after the Hg2+ addition, were compared. It was observed that the absorption peaks of CDs did not shift or disappear significantly before and after the addition of Hg2+, which indicated that the structures of the CDs were not distorted, with no covalent interaction between the CDs and Hg2+. The XPS survey spectrum of CDs in Fig. 1d showed three primary peaks at 284.08, 496.12 and 531.37 eV, which were consistent with C1s, O1s and Na1s respectively. The atomic ratios of carbon, oxygen and sodium in CDs are calculated to be 56.77 %, 21.65 % and 21.57 %, respectively. The deconvolution of high resolution XPS spectra of C1s showed three peaks at 284.8, 286.51 and 288.15 eV. (Fig. 1e) These peaks represented the existence of CC, CO and CO bonds, respectively. As seen in Fig. 1f, O1s has two different binding energies, which belong to CO (530.62 eV) and CO (535.09 eV). It showed that there were several hydroxyl groups present on the surface of the CDs. These XPS results are in agreement with the FTIR results. The Zeta potential of the CDs was measured as negatively charged, and increased when the Hg2+ ions were added, indicating that there was an electrostatic interaction between the CDs and the Hg2+ ions (Table S1).

The UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum, illustrating the optical properties of the CDs, are shown in Fig. 2 . In Fig. 2a, the CDs displayed a clear absorption peak at 262 nm, which was assigned to the π → π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains. The aqueous CDs solution exhibited bright yellow fluorescence under the irradiation of a 365 nm UV lamp (inset in Fig. 2a). At the same time, the maximum fluorescence emission intensity of the CDs peaked at 543 nm at an optimum excitation wavelength of 480 nm (Fig. 2b). The fluorescence spectra of the aqueous CDs solution showed an excitation wavelength of independent character, exhibiting no significant shift in the fluorescence peak at 543 nm for different excitation wavelengths. This suggests that the surface properties of the CDs were relatively uniform [42]. To evaluate the stability of CDs, the solution of CDs and sodium fluorescein was irradiated under a 20 mW·cm−2 xenon lamp for 1 h. Compared with fluorescein disodium salt solution, the absorbance of CDs did not decrease significantly (Fig. S3). When CDs were mixed with different concentrations of NaCl solutions, as shown in Fig. S4, the ionic strength of the medium had no adverse effect on the fluorescence emission and absorption of CDs. These showed that the CDs have certain photostability and chemical stability.

The sensitivity of CDs to Hg2+ ions was evaluated by adding different concentrations of Hg2+ ions. As exhibited in the Fig. 2c, the higher the concentration of Hg2+ ions, the weaker the fluorescence of the CDs. The concentration of Hg2+ ions displayed a linear relationship with the fluorescence intensity ratio of (F0-F)/F0 (Fig. 2d). The linear relationship is mathematically expressed as y = 0.01437x-0.01693. According to the limit of detection(LOD) = 3 δ/k, the detection line of Hg2+ ions is calculated to be 1.6 μM. Comparing the obtained LOD values with some of the recently published data, as shown in Table S2, our results are comparable to or better than many published results. The absorption spectra of the CDs after adding Hg2+ ions were also recorded (Fig. S5). It can be observed that the absorption spectra of CDs are different from those of CDs combined with Hg2+ ions, indicating that a static quenching effect (SQE) process occurred between the CDs and Hg2+ ions [43]. For the purpose of better understanding of the interaction between the CDs and Hg2+, the fluorescence lifetimes of the CDs before and after adding Hg2+ ions were measured, and the values were 4.4468 ns and 4.3080 ns, respectively (Fig. S6). The fluorescence lifetime was somewhat independent of the Hg2+ addition, which indicated the static quenching [44].

Due to the evaluation of the quenching selectivity and anti-interference ability of the CDs, different kinds of metal ions were added into the aqueous CDs solution. As in Fig. 3 a, the quenching ability of Hg2+ ions was much more effective than other metal ions at the same concentration level. In addition, despite the presence of other interfering metal ions, it was observed that the fluorescence quenching ability of Hg2+ ions was still not weakened (Fig. 3b). Thus, the unique performance of the CDs in Hg2+ detection was further confirmed.

We measured the time responses of three concentrations of Hg2+ ions. As shown in Fig. 3c and Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased rapidly within 40 s when Hg2+ was added and these results were the same under different pH conditions (Fig. S8). Simultaneously, we compared the effects of different pH values on the CDs (Fig. 3d). Under neutral condition, fluorescence of the CDs was strongest, while the quenching degree of Hg2+ ions was at maximum under the same condition. In addition, the smaller the pH value, the wider the absorption (Fig. S8i), which was due to the reduced particle size of the CDs under acidic conditions, resulting in the widening of particle size distribution [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-Hg2+ for sensing cysteine as a fluorescence turn-on sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the response of CDs-Hg2+ to Cysteine, various concentrations of Cysteine were mixed with CDs-Hg2+ solution. Thereupon, a gradual recovery of fluorescence intensity was observed (Fig. 4 a), developing a relationship between F0-F/F0 of CDs-Hg2+ solution and the concentration of Cysteine. Besides, the LOD was 0.80 μM (Fig. 4b). Comparing the obtained LOD values with some of the recently published data, as shown in Table S3, our results were comparable to or better than many published results. Due to Cysteine can chelate Hg2+ ions by forming Hg-S bonds, which re-ignite the fluorescence of CDs-Hg2+ after adding cysteine-containing thiol groups. After adding Cysteine, the absorption spectrum revealed a significant change compared with those without adding cysteine, indicating that other substances are formed (Fig. 4c). As shown in Fig. S9, the absorption curve changed obviously after the combination of mercury and cysteine. It can be inferred that only compounds containing mercaptans will cause fluorescence enhancement, such as not only Cys but also Hcy and GSH (Fig. 4d). Fig. 4e shows the time response of cysteine to turn on the fluorescence of CDs-Hg2+ system, which was rapid and stable for restoring the fluorescence intensity. In addition, due to the presence of several amino acids found in natural substances and the body that can interfere with Cysteine detection, the anti-interference ability of CDs-Hg2+ to detect cysteine was tested. The presence of other amino acids did not have a negative impact on the recovery rate of PL caused by Cysteine (Fig. 4f). Moreover, in order to confirm the practicability of CDs-Hg2+ for detecting Cys, different concentrations of Cys were added to serum (Fig. S10). As shown in Table S4, the recoveries of these labeled samples ranged from 84.83 % to 101.5 %. The above results suggest that CDs-Hg2+ can be a “turn-on” sensor for sensing cysteine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the practicability of CDs as probes for detecting Hg2+ ions, different concentrations of Hg2+ ions were added to samples of tap water and lake water (Fig. S11). As shown in Table 1 , the recovery ratesin water and tap water were calculated within the range of 100.80–123.77 % with relative standard deviations (RSD) equal to or less than 1.60 %. This demonstrates the potential of the CDs as sensing systems for detecting Hg2+ ions in natural samples.

The yellow fluorescent CDs were synthesized from kaempferol, a natural carbon source, following a green route. The hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs can interact with Hg2+ ions to induce fluorescence quenching. The as-prepared CDs exhibited excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability for Hg2+ ions, and also had exceptional recovery rates in actual samples. In addition, the fluorescence of CDs-Hg2+ was restored with Cysteine addition. Thus, the CDs could be used as bifunctional detectors of Hg2+ ions and cysteine in mineral waters and the body.

Dan Tang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Qin Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Shaojing Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology. Jianing Yi: Resources, Methodology. Minhuan Lan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Validation. Jie Zeng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Validation. Lei Huang: Supervision, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant number 62175262]; the Hunan Provincial Nature Science Foundation of China [grant number 2022JJ30335]; the Key R & D plan of Hunan Province [grant number 2022SK2101]; the Basic Research Foundation of Shenzhen Science and Technology Innovation[grant number JCYJ20190806144605441]; and the Doctoral Fund Project of Hunan Provincial People's Hospital [grant number BSJJ202107].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2023.111160.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent Progress in Functional Materials for Selective Detection and Removal of Mercury(II) Ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Dai, J. Yang, Y. Wang, Y. Yang, Recent Progress in Functional Materials for Selective Detection and Removal of Mercury(II) Ions, Adv. Funct. Mater. 31 (2021) 2006168. 10.1002/adfm.202006168.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biothiol-Functionalized Cuprous Oxide Sensor for Dual-Mode Sensitive Hg 2+ Detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Pang, H. Bai, H. Zhao, Y. Liu, F. Qin, X. Han, W. Fan, W. Shi, Biothiol-Functionalized Cuprous Oxide Sensor for Dual-Mode Sensitive Hg 2+ Detection, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 13 (2021) 46980–46989. 10.1021/acsami.1c10260.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of metal nanoclusters and their application in Hg2+ ions detection: A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Panthi, M. Park, Synthesis of metal nanoclusters and their application in Hg2+ ions detection: A review, J Haz. Mat. 424 (2022) 127565. 10.1016/j.jhazmat.2021.127565.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: DNAzyme-based biosensors for mercury (Ⅱ) detection: Rational construction, advances and perspectives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Cheng, J. Wei, L. Gu, L. Zou, T. Wang, L. Chen, Y. Li, Y. Yang, P. Li, DNAzyme-based biosensors for mercury (Ⅱ) detection: Rational construction, advances and perspectives, J Haz. Mat. 431 (2022) 128606. 10.1016/j.jhazmat.2022.128606.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mercury sources, contaminations, mercury cycle, detection and treatment techniques: A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.G. Pavithra, P. SundarRajan, P.S. Kumar, G. Rangasamy, Mercury sources, contaminations, mercury cycle, detection and treatment techniques: A review, Chemosphere. 312 (2023) 137314. 10.1016/j.chemosphere.2022.137314.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Domestic water and accumulating mercury toxicity in the kidney

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Abdeldayem, Domestic water and accumulating mercury toxicity in the kidney, Appl Water Sci. 12 (2022) 114. 10.1007/s13201-021-01551-w.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Needle hub in-syringe solid phase extraction based a novel functionalized biopolyamide for simultaneous green separation/preconcentration and determination of cobalt, nickel, and chromium (III) in food and environmental samples with micro sampling flame atomic absorption spectrometry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Shirani, F. Salari, S. Habibollahi, A. Akbari, Needle hub in-syringe solid phase extraction based a novel functionalized biopolyamide for simultaneous green separation/preconcentration and determination of cobalt, nickel, and chromium (III) in food and environmental samples with micro sampling flame atomic absorption spectrometry, Microchem. J. 152 (2020) 104340. 10.1016/j.microc.2019.104340.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green and simple turn off/on fluorescence sensor for mercury (II), cysteine and histidine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Tabaraki, O. Abdi, Green and simple turn off/on fluorescence sensor for mercury (II), cysteine and histidine, J Mol. Liq. 251 (2018) 77–82. 10.1016/j.molliq.2017.12.047.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of Mercury Species in Fish and Seafood by Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry: Validation Study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.E. León-Pérez, A.M. Muñoz-Jiménez, C. Jiménez-Cartagena, Determination of Mercury Species in Fish and Seafood by Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry: Validation Study, Food Anal. Methods. 8 (2015) 2383–2391. 10.1007/s12161-015-0120-z.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Anodic stripping voltammetry with gold electrodes as an alternative method for the routine determination of mercury in fish. Comparison with spectroscopic approaches

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Giacomino, A., Redda, A.R., Squadrone, S., Rizzi, M., Abete, M.C., La Gioia, C., Toniolo, R., Abollino, O. and Malandrino, M., Anodic stripping voltammetry with gold electrodes as an alternative method for the routine determination of mercury in fish. Comparison with spectroscopic approaches, Food Chem. 221 (2017) 737–745. 10.1016/j.foodchem.2016.11.111.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent probes for biomolecule detection under environmental stress

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Han, Y. Wang, Y. Huang, X. Wang, J. Choo, L. Chen, Fluorescent probes for biomolecule detection under environmental stress, J Haz. Mat. 431 (2022) 128527. 10.1016/j.jhazmat.2022.128527.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbazole-thiophene based fluorescent probe for selective detection of Cu2+ and its live cell imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wang, P. Wang, L. Niu, C. Liu, Y. Xiao, Y. Tang, Y. Chen, Carbazole-thiophene based fluorescent probe for selective detection of Cu2+ and its live cell imaging, Spectrochim Acta A. 278 (2022) 121257. 10.1016/j.saa.2022.121257.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent probe for mercury ion imaging analysis: Strategies and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, L. Zhang, X. Han, L. Zhang, X. Wang, L. Chen, Fluorescent probe for mercury ion imaging analysis: Strategies and applications, Chem. Eng. J. 406 (2021) 127166. 10.1016/j.cej.2020.127166.
